commands.review_summary:
The PR adds an observer-only `openclaw doctor` cron advisory for persisted jobs whose concrete announce delivery channel is not active, plus focused collector tests.

PR surface: Source +119, Tests +124. Total +243 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main resolves explicit cron announce delivery targets at run time but the doctor cron pass only emits existing model/legacy notes, so the missing pre-run advisory is visible from code inspection. I did not run a current-main CLI repro because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: - No ClawSweeper repair is indicated because the review found no narrow actionable patch defect; remaining action is normal maintainer and CI review.

target.title: feat(doctor): warn when cron delivery targets inactive channel
target.body:
Related: #97496 (generic channel-plugin activation blockers — complementary, does not inspect cron jobs)

## What Problem This Solves

Resolves a problem where a persisted cron job that pins a concrete `delivery.channel`
(for example `slack`) keeps that target after an update or plugin change deactivates the
channel plugin, then **silently fails to deliver at its next scheduled run** with no
warning ahead of time. `openclaw doctor` already inspects the cron store (legacy
migration, `payload.model` overrides) but never checks whether each job's concrete
delivery target still maps to an active channel plugin, so the drift is invisible until
the run fails.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds an observer-only doctor advisory: when the cron store is inspected, each job whose
resolved primary delivery plan names a **concrete** announce channel is checked against
the active channel plugins for the current config, and a `Cron` note lists any whose
plugin is not active. It is detection only — it never repairs jobs, reactivates channels,
or writes config. Pseudo/relative targets are intentionally skipped because they resolve
at run time and cannot be validated ahead: announce-to-`last`, `webhook`, `none`, and
jobs with no explicit delivery object. The concrete-vs-pseudo decision reuses the existing
`resolveCronDeliveryPlan`, and availability reuses `listReadOnlyChannelPluginsForConfig`
with the same options the doctor channel checks use (`includeSetupFallbackPlugins: true`),
so configured channels are recognized even when no gateway is running and are never
mistaken for unavailable ones. Channel resolution is lazy (only when a job pins a concrete
channel) and best-effort (a resolution failure emits no advisory), so the common doctor
path pays no extra cost and the check never breaks the cron flow. The shared
`formatProviderCounts` helper is generalized to `formatSortedCounts` now that two
advisories use it.

## User Impact

Operators running `openclaw doctor` now get an early, actionable warning when a scheduled
cron job points at a channel whose plugin is no longer active — for example after removing
a channel or changing plugins — instead of discovering the silent delivery failure only
after the next run is missed. The note names the affected job(s) and channel(s) and points
to `openclaw cron list` / `openclaw cron show <job-id>` to reactivate the plugin or update
the target. Jobs that deliver to `last`, via webhook, or with no channel are unaffected, and
when every concrete target resolves to an active channel doctor prints nothing new.

## Evidence

Observer-only; no config schema, CLI flag, or persisted data-model change.

Focused tests (10 new collector tests + 30 existing doctor-cron tests, non-breaking wiring):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
    src/commands/doctor/cron/warnings.test.ts \
    src/commands/doctor/cron/index.test.ts --reporter=dot
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  40 passed (40)
```

Typecheck (core prod + core test lanes): 0 errors.

```
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json ...            # 0 errors
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json ...  # 0 errors
```

Real production-path run — the real `listReadOnlyChannelPluginsForConfig` resolver, the
real `resolveCronDeliveryPlan`, and the real `note` emit, driven against an isolated
`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` with telegram configured and slack absent (redacted token / temp paths;
no mocks):

```
resolved available channel ids (real resolver): ["telegram"]

◇  Cron ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│  Cron delivery targets unavailable channels at <state>/cron/jobs.sqlite.│
│  - 1 job announces to a channel whose plugin is not active; the next    │
│    scheduled run will fail to deliver                                    │
│  - Channels: slack=1                                                     │
│  - Examples: weekly-report -> slack                                     │
│  Reactivate the channel plugin or update the job's `delivery.channel`   │
│  after reviewing with openclaw cron list and openclaw cron show <job-id>.│
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

The same run leaves the configured `telegram` job, an announce-to-`last` job, and a webhook
job unflagged, confirming concrete-vs-pseudo classification on the real path.
target.author: masatohoshino
